primary_diagnosis.name: Acute diffuse otitis externa of the right ear, bacterial, severe/exacerbated

differential.evidence_basis: Ear canal itch prodrome then severe pain, purulent otorrhoea, pain on ear movement — classic external-canal infection.
Also causes otalgia and discharge (if perforated), but usually preceded by URTI and NOT markedly worse with pinna traction.
Invasive skull-base infection to be excluded in diabetics/immunocompromised or with granulation, deep boring pain, or cranial neuropathy.

differential.diagnosis: contact dermatitis of the ear canal
differential.evidence_basis: Can cause itch and canal inflammation, but not thick purulent discharge with severe acute pain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: On history alone otitis externa leads; otitis media and the necrotising mimic must be actively screened.

differential.evidence_basis: Pinna-traction/tragal pain, purulent otorrhoea, canal-centred symptoms; no systemic toxicity, no diabetes/immunosuppression.
No diabetes/immunosuppression, no systemic toxicity, no granulation tissue or cranial-nerve signs reported — low probability, retained only as a safety-net.
Absent URTI prodrome and the hallmark pinna-traction tenderness point to the external canal, not the middle ear.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Screening negative for systemic toxicity and immunosuppression down-weights the necrotising mimic; otitis externa is confirmed clinically.

differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive telehealth diagnosis on classic history and patient-observable findings; definitive otoscopy is a clinician step but does not change first-line management.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth-presumptive; topical therapy plus safety-netting is correct without needing otoscopy first.

red_flags.description: Spreading erythema/swelling behind the ear over the mastoid, or the pinna pushed forward/down — suggests mastoiditis or peri-auricular cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Extension beyond the canal to bone/soft tissue needs urgent in-person assessment and possible IV antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to mastoiditis, abscess or intracranial spread if not escalated.

red_flags.description: Deep, boring, disproportionate pain with facial weakness or other cranial-nerve signs, especially in a diabetic/immunocompromised patient — necrotising (malignant) otitis externa
red_flags.clinical_significance: Invasive skull-base osteomyelitis; high morbidity/mortality, needs urgent ENT and imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Skull-base osteomyelitis and cranial-nerve palsies if not recognised and escalated.

red_flags.description: High-grade fever, rigors, systemic unwellness, neck stiffness, confusion or focal neurological deficit
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests systemic or intracranial spread of infection.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of sepsis or intracranial complication.

pathophysiology_summary: Acute diffuse otitis externa is a bacterial (commonly Pseudomonas aeruginosa or Staphylococcus aureus) infection of the external auditory canal skin, typically triggered by disruption of the protective cerumen/epithelial barrier (moisture, scratching/itch, trauma). Inflammation produces canal oedema, erythema and purulent otorrhoea, with severe pain because the canal skin is tightly bound to perichondrium and bone; pain is characteristically provoked by pinna traction and tragal pressure, and canal swelling causes conductive hearing loss. First-line treatment is topical antibiotic +/- corticosteroid ear drops with aural toileting and analgesia; systemic antibiotics are reserved for spreading cellulitis, immunocompromise, or necrotising disease. The important-not-to-miss complication is necrotising (malignant) otitis externa, an invasive skull-base osteomyelitis seen chiefly in diabetics and the immunocompromised.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Equating severe, distressing, analgesia-refractory pain with a medical emergency and escalating to ED/urgent in-person, when the condition is a common infection manageable with prompt prescription drops and better analgesia.
Advising only paracetamol/keep-dry and failing to arrange prescription topical antibiotic/steroid drops, leaving a severe infection under-treated.
Not screening for diabetes/immunosuppression and cranial-nerve signs, and so failing to safety-net for necrotising otitis externa.

Prescribing oral antibiotics for uncomplicated otitis externa — topical therapy achieves far higher local concentrations and oral antibiotics are not indicated unless infection spreads beyond the canal.
Suggesting the patient clean the canal with cotton buds, which traumatises canal skin and worsens the infection.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Otoscopic view of the deep canal and tympanic membrane (canal erythema/oedema restricting visibility, TM integrity)
Direct palpation eliciting tragal tenderness and preauricular lymphadenopathy
Confirmation of absence of granulation tissue in the canal floor
Objective vital signs without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation required at baseline: recognise otitis externa, arrange prompt prescription topical antibiotic/steroid ear drops and optimised analgesia via a GP or telehealth prescriber within 24-48 hours, give ear-care and safety-net advice, and escalate to same-day in-person/ED only if a red flag (RF-001/002/003) emerges.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low — the history is classic and management is topical regardless of otoscopy. The real risks are directional triage errors: over-triaging the severe pain to ED, or under-treating with self-care only.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Topical antibiotic +/- corticosteroid ear drops with aural toilet and analgesia are first-line for acute otitis externa; oral antibiotics are reserved for spreading infection or immunocompromise; suspect necrotising otitis externa in diabetics/immunocompromised with severe pain.
